input.background: Refer to Example 9.10. Suppose the same factorial experiment is performed on four other brands (E, F, G, and H), and the results are as shown in Table 9.16. Repeat the factorial analysis and interpret the results.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Table 9.16 & \multicolumn{5}{|r|}{Distance Data for Second Factorial Golf Experiment} \\
\hline \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{} & \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{Brand} \\
\hline & & E & F & G & H \\
\hline Club & Driver & 238.6 & 261.4 & 264.7 & 235.4 \\
\hline & & 241.9 & 261.3 & 262.9 & 239.8 \\
\hline & & 236.6 & 254.0 & 253.5 & 236.2 \\
\hline & & 244.9 & 259.9 & 255.6 & 237.5 \\
\hline & & & & & \\
\hline & Five-Iron & 165.2 & 179.2 & 189.0 & 171.4 \\
\hline & & 156.9 & 171.0 & 191.2 & 159.3 \\
\hline & & 172.2 & 178.0 & 191.3 & 156.6 \\
\hline & & 163.2 & 182.7 & 180.5 & 157.4 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Determine which of the Brands' mean distances differ using Tukey's method. What are the results of the pairwise comparisons?

output.answer: Using Tukey's method at α = 0.10, Minitab computes simultaneous 90% confidence intervals for the six possible comparisons of the form μi - μj. Any interval that does not include 0 implies a significant difference between the two treatment means. Brands G and F are associated with significantly greater mean distances than Brands E and H, but we cannot distinguish between Brands G and F or between Brands E and H.
